entry.title: Issue with Inbound Mail Processing in SAP
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We are facing an issue related to inbound mail processing in our SAP system.

We have already checked the following points:

  • SCOT configuration
  • SMICM service status
  • SICF service activation
  • User creation in SU01 with user type “System”
  • Network connectivity between SAP and mail server

Earlier, we were facing issues with outgoing mails as well; however, the outbound mail processing is now working properly. Despite all checks being completed successfully and no network issues being observed, inbound mails are still not being processed in SAP.

Request you to please help us identify the root cause and suggest the required solution.

Please let us know if any additional logs, traces, or configuration details are required from our end.

Regards,
Chetan Chavan

entry.title: SAP Joule for Consultants Gets Personal at SAP Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
What's New: Personal and Organizational Knowledge in SAP Joule for Consultants

We've introduced a set of capabilities that let consultants and consulting organizations enrich SAP Joule with customer-specific content across three complementary approaches. Critically — none of these files are ever used to train the underlying LLM. Your content stays yours.

  • File upload at prompt level
  • File upload at Expert Workspace
  • Custom Knowledge Grounding

1. File Upload — Quick Context at the Prompt Level

The simplest way to give Joule relevant context is to upload a file directly within a conversation.

  • Scope: User level, single conversation
  • How it works: Upload a file at the prompt level and Joule uses it as context for that session
  • Storage: SAP Joule for Consultants managed storage; files are automatically deleted after 7 days
  • Data type: PDF, TXT, WORD, Images

This is ideal for ad-hoc tasks — analyzing a client's as-is process document, reviewing a project charter, or grounding a response in a specific contract or specification. These are typically files you have at hand: living documents that are being actively revised, drafts, client-specific outputs that change from engagement to engagement. No setup required, no permanence needed.

2. File Upload at Expert Workspace — Persistent Context for Your Focus Area

For consultants who repeatedly work in a specific domain or with a recurring client, the Expert Workspace takes file upload a step further. Details in SAP Help.

  • Scope: User level / Expert level
  • How it works: Files are uploaded directly into an Expert Workspace, making them persistently available across conversations within that workspace
  • Data type: PDF, TXT, WORD, Images

Think of an Expert Workspace as your specialized "cockpit" for a given area of work — whether that's S/4HANA Finance, SAP SuccessFactors, or a specific customer engagement. Files you upload here might change more frequently than organizational standards — evolving project artifacts, updated configuration specs, or a client's latest process documentation. You're in control of when to swap them out.

3. Custom Knowledge Grounding — Enterprise-Scale, Admin-Managed

For consulting organizations that want to make standardized knowledge available across their entire team, Custom Knowledge Grounding is the answer.

  • Scope: Tenant/Instance level — available across every conversation (admin-controlled on/off)
  • Connects to: SharePoint, Amazon S3, SAP Build Work Zone
  • Storage: Managed in AI Core by the customer — no automatic deletion
  • Support data types and data repository: Details in this Document Grounding SAP Help page.

  • Detailed can be found in SAP Help page
  • Step by Step guide in this community blog post.
  • Support data types and data repository can be found in this Document Grounding SAP Help page.

What Makes a Good Document for Custom Knowledge Grounding?

Since admins control the sync cycle and this content underpins every conversation on the tenant, it's worth being intentional about what you put there. Here are the best practices we recommend:

Choose documents with real operational knowledge:

  • Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and process descriptions
  • Configuration guides and technical standards
  • Customizations and extension documentation
  • Internal business rules
  • Troubleshooting steps and known-issue resolutions

These are the materials that will meaningfully influence Joule's answers — not general SAP documentation (which Joule already knows), but the things that are specific to your organization or delivery methodology.

Structure content for best retrieval performance:

Keep documents modular and scoped. Smaller, topic-specific files index better than large all-in-one documents. Instead of uploading one 200-page User Manual, break it apart:

  • "User Role Setup Guide"
  • "Custom Fields Documentation"
  • "Approval Workflow Rules"

Each file becomes a more precise retrieval unit, which means Joule can surface the right answer with higher accuracy.

Keep documents clean. Avoid:

  • Scanned images and "image-only" PDFs — text cannot be extracted from these
  • Watermarks and boilerplate legal pages — they add noise without signal
  • Redundant or duplicated content across files — this dilutes retrieval quality

The underlying principle: cleaner text → better embeddings → more accurate retrieval. Investing a little effort in document hygiene before upload pays dividends in every single Joule response that draws on that content.

Matching the Right Approach to the Right Content

A practical way to think about it: each method has a natural content "temperature."

 File Upload (Prompt) File Upload (Expert Workspace)Custom Knowledge Grounding
Who manages itIndividual userIndividual userAdmin only
Update frequencyAny timeAny timeAdmin-triggered sync
Content typeFiles at hand, in-progress drafts, client-specific outputsFrequently updated domain or project filesStable, authoritative org knowledge
ScopeSingle conversationExpert WorkspaceEntire tenant
Persistence7 daysWorkspace lifetimeNo deletion (customer-managed)
Best forAd-hoc contextDomain/client focusOrganization-wide standards

 

Privacy and Trust: A Non-Negotiable

One principle cuts across all approaches: files uploaded via any of these methods are never used to train the LLM. Your client data, your intellectual property, your methodology — none of it feeds back into model training. We follow SAP AI Ethics and as FAQ below of this site "SAP stands by the security of customer data and does not share customer data with other vendors for the purpose of training or improving their large language models (LLMs) or image models.", and we at SAP know how important it matters deeply to consulting organizations operating under strict data governance requirements.

entry.title: New release: X-Rechnung support for Germany on SAP Business Network
entry.content:
Germany’s e-invoice mandate

Germany is moving toward mandatory e-invoicing requirements, effective January 2027. This means businesses must be able to issue and process invoices in the required structured format.

Without proper support, this introduces compliance risks, manual effort, and potential disruptions in invoicing processes. Late preparation often leads to rejected invoices, delayed payments, and penalties.

Early readiness is key.

SAP Business Network is compliant with X-Rechnung invoices

To respond to that, SAP Business Network introduces support for X-Rechnung invoices in Germany in the 2605 release, helping businesses prepare for the upcoming German e-invoicing regulations. Adopting X-Rechnung now allows organizations to align with regulatory requirements ahead of time and avoid last-minute changes.

With the new feature, SAP Business Network supports the creation, validation, and signing of X-Rechnung invoices for domestic transactions. X-Rechnung compliance is built directly into the invoicing process. Once enabled by the buyer, suppliers can create invoices that are automatically validated against the required structure and data rules.

In more detail, the X-Rechnung file serves as the legal invoice, while the cXML format is used for data transfer and processing. The feature also supports invoice archiving for better reporting. Please note that while the feature is generally available, it requires activation by the buyer.

You can access the documentation here: https://help.sap.com/docs/buying-invoicing/sap-business-network-guide-to-invoicing/x-rechnung-support-for-germany-9c68bf55f1cf4216996af83d1b065336?q=germany

What other benefits should you expect

SAP Business Network ensures invoices meet compliance requirements before submission, reducing errors and rejections. On top of that, buyer-defined business rules still apply, helping prevent suppliers from submitting incorrect invoices that are against the buyer’s policies. Thus, the process remains consistent within the same workspace, avoiding workarounds.

The result is straightforward: compliant invoices, supported archiving, and a smoother transition to Germany’s 2027 e-invoicing mandate.

entry.title: Re: Need Clarification For EKORG
entry.content:
Central Purchasing OrganizationUtilize a single purchasing organization assigned to multiple company codes. Create separate RFQs per company code but manage them centrally.Centralized control, standardized SAP processStill multiple RFQs, not a single consolidated document
Collective RFQ / Reference DocumentCreate one "reference RFQ" manually (outside SAP or via document management) and replicate it into SAP for each company code.Vendors see a single consolidated requestManual effort, duplication of data in SAP
Custom Development / ExtensionDevelop a custom application or enhancement to generate a single RFQ covering multiple company codes, then split it internally into individual SAP RFQs.Meets management's requirement for a "single RFQ"Non-standard approach, requires development and ongoing support
Framework Agreement / ContractNegotiate a central contract with the supplier at the purchasing organization level, then issue RFQs/POs per company code while referencing the central contract.Legally compliant, centralized negotiationStill multiple RFQs in SAP, but unified contract

entry.title: Evolving Our SAP Signavio Value Accelerators: Announcing Upcoming Content Updates Q2 2026
entry.content:
At SAP Signavio, we are committed to evolving our solutions to provide you with the most powerful and user-friendly tools for continuous business process transformation. A key part of this commitment is continuously enhancing and consolidating our portfolio of value accelerators for SAP Signavio Process Intelligence.

 

From Starter Content to End-to-End Intelligence

Over the past years, we have been hard at work developing new, state-of-the-art content that goes far beyond the original “Start Mining” accelerator series. We have engineered richer data pipelines, more comprehensive persona-centric dashboards, and deeply integrated end-to-end process coverage across SCM and procurement domains.

As we introduce these more powerful assets, we have identified an overlap with a number of older value accelerators. To reduce complexity for you and focus our engineering efforts on the best possible content, we will be retiring the overlapping packages in November 2026.

 

What’s Changing?

Here is a breakdown of the upcoming removals, organized by process area.

 

Packages Being Retired: Warehouse Management

The following value accelerators are set for removal from the value accelerator library in November 2026, as their functionality is now more effectively covered by the new Embedded SAP Extended Warehouse Management accelerators:

  • Start Mining for Managing Warehouse and Inventory (Outbound) in SAP Extended Warehouse Management
  • Improve On-Time Delivery Performance for Managing Warehouse (Outbound) in SAP Extended Warehouse Management
  • Reduce Revenue Loss Due to Stock-outs for Managing Warehouse (Outbound) in SAP Extended Warehouse Management

Replaced by:

  • Identify Improvement Potential for Embedded SAP Extended Warehouse Management (Outbound)
  • Improve Process Performance for SAP Extended Warehouse Management (Outbound)

These new accelerators are already available in the value accelerator library.

 

Packages Being Retired: Source Products and Services, and Procure to Pay (SAP Ariba)

The following value accelerators are set for removal in November 2026, as the unified end-to-end Source to Pay pipeline now provides comprehensive coverage:

  • Start Mining for Source Products and Services in SAP Ariba
  • Start Mining for Procure to Pay in SAP Ariba

Replaced by:

  • Identify Improvement Potential for Source to Pay in SAP Ariba
  • Improve Process Performance for Source to Pay in SAP Ariba

This accelerator delivers an integrated end-to-end view across sourcing, contracting, and procurement in a single pipeline with dedicated dashboards.

 

Package Being Retired: Vendor Invoice Management

The following value accelerator is set for removal in November 2026, as dedicated Invoice to Pay accelerators now provide deeper, more targeted coverage:

  • Start Mining for Vendor Invoice Management supported by SAP Invoice Management by OpenText

Replaced by:

  • Invoice Excellence for Invoice to Pay Supported by OpenText in SAP ECC
  • Invoice Excellence for Invoice to Pay Supported by OpenText in SAP S/4HANA

These replacements are already available in the value accelerator library.

 

Impact and Your Next Steps

We want to make this transition as smooth as possible. Here is what you need to know:

What about my existing content? If you have already downloaded any of the affected packages, you can continue to use your local installations without any issues. The removal only affects the availability of these packages for new downloads from the value accelerator library after November 2026.

How do I handle customizations? We understand that many of you have customized your pipelines and dashboards. You can continue using your customized content in the existing assets, or migrate your customizations to the new accelerators. A comprehensive migration guide is available to help you with this process: Migration of Customized Content.

Our Maintenance Commitment: Going forward, our primary maintenance and bug-fix efforts will be focused on the new and updated content available in the value accelerator library. After November 2026, maintenance for the removed packages will be discontinued. If a bug is reported in content that is no longer in the library, we will provide a solution for the customer and apply the fix to the corresponding KPIs within the new, actively maintained value accelerators.

 

Timeline

To give you ample time to adapt, we have established a clear transition path:

  • Now – October 31, 2026: Transition period. The older value accelerators remain available for download from the value accelerator library.
  • November 2026: The retired value accelerators will be removed from the library, and engineering maintenance on them will be discontinued.

 

We’re Here to Help

We are confident these changes will provide a more powerful, streamlined, and insightful experience for analyzing your business processes. The new accelerators bring richer pipelines, end-to-end visibility, and persona-focused dashboards that go well beyond what the retiring packages offered.

For detailed instructions on migrating your custom content, please refer to our help page: Migration of Customized Content.

Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact us or your SAP Signavio representative.

Thank you for being a valued customer.

 

SAP Signavio Product Management Team

entry.title: Re: Display item of content page on master page in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello Shubham

I did 3 errors in my previous post.

1. The script must be at the "LAYOUT:READY" event not "form:ready".

2. I forgot to mention the proposed script is in Formcalc script

3.  Pages references:

    mst1 => Refer to the first page

     xfa.resolveNode("#pageSet[0].mst1.SubsequentPage.frmHeader.FormTitle") = $

   mst2 => Refer to subsequence pages

    xfa.resolveNode("#pageSet[0].mst2.SubsequentPage.frmHeader.FormTitle") = $

Normally using only:  xfa.resolveNode("#pageSet[0].mst1.SubsequentPage.frmHeader.FormTitle") = $

Will automatically be transmitted to the subsequence pages without having to add the "mst2" code.

Regards

entry.title: Re: Packing Instruction - Reference Material
entry.content:
Good day @PedroCarvalho14 

Thank you for your question.

The reference material is intended to reduce packing instruction maintenance when many products are packed in the same way. You use the Manage Product Master Data app to                                                                                                                     

create a reference material for packing, assign it in the product master of the materials packed identically and then create one packing instruction for the reference material.   

However, the reference material is only used in automatic packaging if the active packing instruction determination searches by Reference Material. Packing instruction determination uses condition technique and the access sequence defines which condition table fields are searched for a valid determination record. If the active key combination in POF1 is based on Material, then the system searches for that actual product number. In that case, maintaining the condition record with the reference material number will not automatically apply it to all materials assigned to that reference material.

As a next step, i suggest you check the POF1 key combination and the access sequence behind 0IBD and 0GRV. If a key combination with Reference Material is available, maintain the condition record there and retest with a real product that has the reference material assigned in the product master. If only Material based key combinations are available, then the system behaviour is expected and you either need material specific condition records or an adjusted determination setup that include Reference Material as a condition field.

See Maintain packing information for more details.

To summarise:

  • You do not need one condition record per material only if the active packing instruction determination uses Reference Material as a condition field.
  • If the active key combination uses Material, then yes, you must maintain the condition record per material or adjust the determination setup to include Reference Material.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: BANK RECONCILIATION METHOD IN SAP S4 HANA PUBLIC CLOUD
entry.content:
Hello Rahul,

Thank you for the update. To give you a direct answer regarding your client's expectations:

  1. Increasing the Field Limit: The "Assignment" field has a strict limit of 18 characters, while the "Reference" field has only a 16 characters limit in the system. In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, these standard system limits are fixed and cannot be increased. (Actually, these limits have been in SAP already for a very long time.)

  2. Text Field Algorithm: There is no standard, pre-delivered interpretation algorithm designed to match the Memo Line directly against the "Item Text" field of an open invoice item. The system is designed to rely on specific reference fields for automated clearing.

My proposed Solutions: Since you cannot change the field lengths or use the Item Text, I am aware of two options depending on the client's business process:

Option 1: Utilize the Payment Reference Field
If the UTR/RTGS reference is up to 30 characters long, the best practice is to map this number into the Payment Reference field during the invoice creation process, rather than using the Assignment or Item Text fields. It might also be achieved by a simple substitution rule created in Manage Substitution and Validation Rules - Journal Entries that derives the payment reference from item text, or any other field where the reference is entered originally.

  • How it works: Once the data is in the Payment Reference field on the open item, you can use standard Processing Instructions (such as 0021 or 0031 for Payment Reference) under your "Clear in Sequence" rule. The system will then successfully match the long UTR number from your uploaded bank statement's Memo Line against the Payment Reference field of the open invoice.

Option 2: Utilize Payment Advice
If the client cannot change their invoice entry process, or if a single UTR number frequently pays multiple invoices at once, you should use Payment Advices. I clearly declare that this is a bit of a complex process and might, in the end, require even more effort from the end users compared to manual clearing.

  • How it works: A Payment Advice can be created in the system before the bank statement is uploaded (either manually via the Manage Payment Advices app, or automatically via API from an external system - that's where potential added value lies, if you were able to automate the creation). The user then simply types the Payment Advice Number into the Memo Line of the Bank Statement Excel template.

  • The Rule: You then configure your processing rule with a Processing Instruction for Payment Advices (such as 0040 or 0045). The system reads the advice number from the Memo Line and automatically clears all open items associated with that advice.

Let me know if one of those approaches might help your client to automate the process a bit.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: AI has outgrown legacy data architectures like SAP BW — is your stack ready to keep up?
entry.content:
Today’s data architecture can no longer just feed dashboards – it has to feed applications, agents, and AI-native products.

For years, our data architecture was optimised for a deterministic world focused on one purpose: feeding applications  
On one side you had operational databases, on the other a data warehouse, with compute in between to move and model data.
The main success metric was price–performance: maximise query throughput while minimising cost. That model worked well, but the world has changed.  AI and agentic workloads do not fit neatly into this static, siloed stack, and traditional pipelines cannot keep up with the speed and context modern business applications require.

We then entered the Lakehouse era, where compute and storage were decoupled and a unified layer emerged across data. This closed some gaps, but it still fell short of what AI-driven applications and agents need today: deep business context, not just accessible data. This is why the notion of a “knowledge core” has become so important.
It provides the shared semantic and business context that powers not only dashboards, but also agents, co-pilots, and operational applications that must understand your processes and decisions. SAP Business Data Cloud is designed exactly for this new reality.
It unifies SAP and non-SAP data, preserves business semantics, and exposes a governed knowledge core that can feed dashboards, intelligent applications, and AI agents from the same trusted foundation. 

SAP Business Data Cloud - the components that make modern, AI-driven business possible

Today's successful data strategies do more than store facts - they connect data to meaning and action. SAP Business Data Cloud delivers that connection as a single, managed service by combining four complementary layers that together form a business data fabric: lake storage, intelligent compute, a knowledge core, and AI-powered agents and applications.

Lake storage: SAP and non-SAP data (including SAP Business Warehouse sources) are brought together into a secure foundation so teams can access the full enterprise dataset without brittle point-to-point integrations. This unified storage lets organisations keep authoritative copies where needed and enable zero-copy access patterns where performance and scale demand.

Intelligent compute: Workloads run where they make most sense - SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Databricks, and SAP Snowflake provide flexible execution across workflows based on your use case.

Knowledge core: Delivered through SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud, the knowledge core adds semantics, curated data products, and knowledge graphs so data carries business context (definitions, lineage, relations). That context is what turns raw datasets into trusted business artefacts that analysts, planners, and ML models can rely on.

AI agents & intelligent applications: Joule and Intelligent Applications surface the knowledge core to people and processes — answering open questions, generating analytics, orchestrating workflows, and executing decisions that are grounded in governed data. Agents draw on prebuilt skills and the knowledge graph to automate work while preserving traceability and control.

Why the integration matters

Individually these components are powerful, but the real value comes from how they work together as a fabric: governed data flows from lake storage into semantic models, compute layers enrich and transform it, the knowledge core captures meaning and relationships, and AI agents turn that context into action. The result is an enterprise-grade foundation for trusted, explainable AI and faster, safer decision-making at scale.

How it feels in practice - Example

A planner asks Joule for the expected impact of a seasonal promotion. The agent retrieves harmonised sales and customer data from the lake, uses a Databricks model for demand forecasting, references product and promotion semantics from the knowledge graph, and returns an explainable recommendation inside an SAP Analytics Cloud story - all while preserving lineage and access controls. That end-to-end flow is what SAP Business Data Cloud is built to enable.

 

Did you know that SAP Datasphere which is part of the knowledge core goes far beyond traditional data warehousing compared with SAP BW and SAP BW/4HANA?

It retains the core capabilities customers know from SAP BW and BW/4HANA - including data integration, data management and governance, analytic modeling, and integrated analytics and planning - so the foundation is not lost, but carried forward.

At the same time, SAP Datasphere expands the scope in three important ways. First, it grows into an application platform through integrated add-ons. Second, it extends into a lakehouse architecture with object storage and a separation of compute and storage, which brings greater elasticity and scalability. Third, it introduces capabilities beyond classic data warehousing, such as self-service spaces, semantic onboarding, a data marketplace, a knowledge graph for GenAI scenarios, and an open ecosystem for technology partner integrations.

The key message is simple: everything customers value in SAP BW today remains relevant, but SAP Datasphere opens the door to a much broader, more flexible, and more future-ready data platform.

 

What is now SAP's Offering to Safeguard investments with a guided modernization strategy?

SAP gives every SAP BW customer a clear, low‑risk path into the future with a three‑track strategy: Optimize, Modernize, Innovate.

  • Optimize keeps your current SAP BW investment safe while you prepare your move. SAP helps you reduce and tier data to cut storage costs, shift BW into a private cloud if you want, and secures your operations with extended SAP BW NetWeaver maintenance as a safety net for customers who need more time.

  • Modernize brings your analytics into SAP Datasphere and SAP Business Data Cloud without starting from scratch. With tools like the Query Template Generator (Name could change), Data Product Generator, and BW Migration Assistant (Name could change), you can systematically move queries, semantic data models, and ETL processes into a modern, cloud‑native setup.

  • Innovate is where you unlock real upside: SAP‑managed data products replace legacy SAP BW workflows, AI agents accelerate data product creation, and tight integration with platforms such as Databricks opens up advanced ML and GenAI scenarios on trusted SAP data.

The best part: these tracks are not a rigid sequence. You can optimize parts of your landscape, modernize priority workloads, and innovate in high‑value areas in parallel - at the pace that fits your organization’s readiness and risk profile.

 

Let's have a closer look into the 3 Toolings & the first one is already available in a second version since a couple of month. 

Tooling 1: Data Product Generator for SAP Business Data Cloud - Already available

The Data Product Generator is the fast lane for bringing your SAP BW data layer into SAP Datasphere. It pushes InfoProvider data straight into the SAP Datasphere object store while keeping SAP BW semantics fully intact, so you can reuse your existing logic instead of rebuilding it.

It supports all key SAP BW InfoProvider types - InfoObjects, DSOs, CompositeProviders, MultiProviders, InfoCubes, and even Queries-as-InfoProviders - preserves associations, and handles both initial loads and delta updates across large SAP BW landscapes. In internal benchmarks, this automation delivers at least an 80% faster path than manual extraction and loading.

In the architecture shown here, the Data Product Generator reads directly from the SAP BW InfoProvider, lands files and delta tables in the SAP BW Inbound Space of the SAP Datasphere object store, and surfaces the result as a ready‑to‑use Data Product with an accompanying Delta Share.

Example of an SAP BW InfoProvider that has been replicated into the object store via subscription – including its full business context and semantics.

 

Tooling 2: Query Template Generator for SAP Business Data Cloud - planned for Q2/Q3 2026 (Tooling Name not final yet (could change))

The Query Template Generator is the engine that moves your analytical workloads from SAP BW into SAP Datasphere without disruption. Its purpose is to automatically migrate SAP BW query metadata and business logic into Analytic Models, so users can continue working with familiar queries while the platform modernizes underneath.

In concrete terms, it converts SAP BW Queries into Analytic Models, transfers all query metadata, and brings along the fact source together with its associated dimensions. SAP’s internal measurements show around a 50% reduction in migration effort compared to manual re‑creation.

The Query Template Generator reads a SAP BW Query with its InfoProvider and InfoObjects and translates it into an Analytic Model with a Fact View, dimensions, hierarchies, and texts — all landing in a dedicated HANA Space in SAP Datasphere. It works hand‑in‑hand with the Data Product Generator, which takes care of the underlying data layer.

 

Tooling 3: BW Migration Assistant for SAP Business Data Cloud - planned for Q4/2026 - Q1/2027 (Tooling Name not final yet (could change))

The BW Migration Assistant is the tool that moves your data pipelines – not just queries or one‑off data extracts, but the full ETL layer. Its purpose is to translate SAP BW data flows into equivalent SAP Datasphere artefacts, with AI handling most of the heavy lifting on ABAP code conversion.

It focuses on SAP BW data flows: transformations, DTPs, and the chained InfoProvider‑to‑InfoProvider structures that form the backbone of typical BW architectures. The AI engine converts ABAP routines into SQL‑based Transformation Flows in SAP Datasphere, and SAP’s internal measurements indicate about a 50% reduction in migration effort compared to manual re‑implementation.

 

Call to Action!

Now is the right moment to move a legacy SAP BW to SAP Business Data Cloud if you want to get serious about AI, for three key reasons:

  1. AI needs live, contextual data – not just historical reports.
    Modern GenAI, agents, and ML models depend on fresh, granular, well‑defined data products. Classic SAP BW is batch‑oriented, tightly coupled, and hard to plug into flexible AI workloads.

  2. SAP BDC turns your SAP BW investment into an AI‑ready asset instead of technical debt.
    You can lift SAP BW models, semantics, and data flows into cloud‑native data products and analytic models, instead of rebuilding everything from scratch. That means faster time‑to‑value and far less migration risk.

  3. There is a real window of opportunity right now.
    Maintenance timelines, cloud strategies, and exploding AI demand are converging. If you modernize SAP BW into SAP BDC now, you create a clean, governed foundation for AI for the next 5–10 years, instead of pouring more effort into a legacy stack that can’t keep up.

In short: legacy SAP BW slows your AI ambitions down; SAP Business Data Cloud gives you a future‑proof, open data foundation where AI can actually scale and deliver measurable business impact.

entry.title: From Cell & Gene to Advanced Therapy: Evolving SAP’s Orchestration Platform for a Broader Future
entry.content:
A Name That Outgrew Itself  

When SAP first introduced its orchestration platform for cell and gene therapies, the category was clearly defined: complex biological medicines requiring end-to-end traceability, time-critical logistics, and patient-level coordination. The product name fit well.  

But advanced therapies does not stand still.  

The platform has been built and proven for cell and gene therapy – but its core capabilities – treatment order management, shipment lifecycle tracking, chain-of-identity and custody traceability, scheduling and end-to-end orchestration, are not exclusive to that therapy type. The same operational complexity that defines CAR-T and gene therapy exists in radioligand therapy, allogeneic programs, plasma-derived biologics, and class 3 medical devices. The platform was always designed with individual capabilities and features woven together using events & micro-services based internal architecture and hence can be the foundation to serve all of them. A name that says "Cell and Gene" no longer reflects that potential.  

What Changes - and What Doesn't  

The name changes. SAP Cell and Gene Therapy Orchestration becomes SAP Advanced Therapy Orchestration.  

The platform itself is not changing. All current features, including treatment order management, shipment lifecycle tracking, COI/COC monitoring and traceability, scheduling, exception management, and S/4HANA integration, remain exactly as they are. The technology, architecture, APIs, and events all stay the same. Existing documentation and technical references will be updated to reflect the new name soon. 

What is changing is the scope of our roadmap and the range of customers we are innovating with SAP AI agents and Joule engagement layer accelerating this evolutionary morphing. 

Why "Advanced Therapy"?  

Advanced therapy is a term widely used in the life sciences industry – formalized by the EMA as Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (ATMPs) — to describe medicines that go beyond traditional small molecules and biologics. They share a common set of operational challenges: supply chain complexity, chain-of-identity tracking, cold-chain logistics, regulatory traceability, and patient-level orchestration. These include: 

  • Cell therapies — autologous (patient-specific) and allogeneic (donor-based)  
  • Gene therapies — viral vector delivery, in vivo and ex vivo approaches  
  • Radioligand therapies — targeted radiopharmaceuticals combining a targeting molecule with a radioactive payload  
  • Combination products — medical devices integrated with biologics  
  • Plasma-derived biologics — derived from donated blood plasma through fractionation  

What unites them is not the science – it is the operational complexity. And that is exactly what the SAP Advanced Therapy Orchestration platform was designed to handle.  

The Expansion Areas  

  1. Medical Device Integration - A Differentiated Opportunity  

Many advanced therapies require medical devices as part of the treatment – injection systems, preparation kits, surgical instruments – shipped separately from the drug product but logically linked to the same patient and procedure. SAP Advanced Therapy Orchestration already supports this correlation through parallel flow steps and treatment orders that link drug products and devices across different shipment types and timescales.                                                   

The roadmap extends this further: supporting the full device lifecycle including kit dispatch, usage tracking, and return logistics for reusable instruments, as well as deeper orchestration of combination products where a device and a biologic are regulatory-linked as a single therapy.  

2. Plasma-Derived Biologics - From Donation to Fractionation  

Plasma-derived biologics share key supply chain characteristics with cell and gene therapies: donation collection, chain-of-identity tracking, complex multi-step processing, and end-to-end traceability from donor to finished product. The difference is scale – plasma fractionation operates at industrial volumes – but the orchestration challenge is the same.  

SAP Advanced Therapy Orchestration plans to extend its proven chain-of-identity and traceability model to cover donation collection and processing orchestration for plasma-derived products.  

3. Allogeneic Therapy - Extending Donation Processing  

SAP Advanced Therapy Orchestration already supports allogeneic therapies – donor-derived, off-the-shelf products manufactured in batches rather than per patient. The roadmap will add donation processing capabilities, covering the early stages when donor material is collected, qualified, and tracked before it enters the manufacturing process. This gives therapy developers and CMOs one platform for the entire allogeneic lifecycle, from donation to patient delivery.  

4. Radioligand Therapy - Extending Treatment Plan Support  

SAP Advanced Therapy Orchestration already supports radioligand therapy – including multiple shipment requests per treatment cycle, tight scheduling windows by isotope half-lives, and cold chain logistics with shelf-lives measured in hours rather than days. The roadmap will add treatment plan capabilities – modeling multi-cycle treatment schedules where a single patient receives multiple doses over weeks or months, each requiring its own manufacturing, logistics, and scheduling coordination. This gives nuclear pharmacy networks and therapy developers the orchestration backbone for dose-level planning across the full course of treatment.  

Why Now? What this Means for You  

The timing is deliberate. After 14 releases, and multiple productive customers with proven deployments across different modalities, the platform has outgrown its original name. For current customers, nothing changes in your environment – your experience, integration, and ongoing support will remain exactly as they are. The transition to the new name happens automatically within your systems.  

For organizations orchestrating radioligand therapies, plasma-derived products, allogeneic programs, or combination devices: the supply chain complexity you are managing today – the scheduling pressure, the traceability requirements, the multi-party coordination – is what this platform was built to solve. We invite you to explore it.

entry.title: Re: Issue with Inbound Mail Processing in SAP
entry.content:
Hello 

Inbound mail issue is usually due to missing/incorrect inbound setup,

Maintain Inbound node in SCOT (Settings → Inbound Processing)
Schedule job RSCONN01 (for inbound processing)
Verify handler class / address mapping (SO50 / CL_BCS_INBOUND)
Check SOST / SOSG logs for inbound errors
Ensure mail server allows inbound fetch (POP3/IMAP)


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: How do upgrade SAP Business Apllication Studio Plan f...
entry.content:
Hello 

Upgrade BAS plan via SAP BTP,

Go to SAP BTP Cockpit → Subaccount → Entitlements
Add/Increase SAP Business Application Studio (Standard Edition)
Assign quota to subaccount
Go to Service Marketplace → BAS → Subscribe (Standard plan)

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Event-driven architecture
entry.content:
The issue is resolved. The key point is that SAP Event Mesh only distributes events — it does not execute business logic or guarantee downstream processing. The correct approach is to let Event Mesh publish the event and then handle all processing in a dedicated consumer (SAP Integration Suite iFlow, Kyma service, CAP application, etc.).

Once I created a proper subscriber and moved the transformation/orchestration logic out of Event Mesh and into the consumer service, the event flow worked end‑to‑end. The event schema, queue subscription, and correlation handling were aligned, and the message was processed reliably.

In short: Event Mesh = transport layer Subscriber service = business logic + processing

entry.title: Output Parameters for Handling Unit
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I’m trying to configure HU printing for the Output Type HU_SHIPPING_LABEL.

My goal is to print the label only when using Pick-HU processing. In my scenario, the Pick-HU is:

  1. Created on the resource
  2. Gradually filled on the resource
  3. Finally placed into the Goods Issue bin   

What I want is to trigger the printing only when the HU is placed in the final destination bin.

The issue is the following:

  • If I use the HU creation step, the HU is still empty, so I do not have any content information available.
  • If I use the HU change/modification step, the output is triggered as soon as the HU is filled. If multiple products are placed into the HU, the label is printed:
    • once after confirming the product warehouse task
    • and a second time after the task that links the stock to the delivery

Additionally, if the HU contains several material/batch combinations, the system prints the full label every time the HU is updated. However, my requirement is to print the label only once, at the very end of the process.

I have already reviewed SAP Note 2941522 and the SAP documentation I could find, but I have not been able to solve it.  https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/36597ffd10024278bdb455c22bfd8c60.html

I also wanted to ask whether there is any way to get a determination log or trace for output determination. Without such a log, troubleshooting feels almost like trial and error, testing endless combinations until something finally works. On top of that, some fields seem to be incompatible with others during condition determination.

Has anyone faced the same issue or knows whether a determination log exists and how to access it?

Thanks in advance.

Vicente Jarque

entry.title: Re: Building Your First SAP Module Pool: A Step-by-Step Guide to Screen Painter & Menu Painter
entry.content:
It's a VERY OLD technology; the comments on this other blog post are still relevant: Module Pool Programming from scratch - SAP Community.

Note that your ABAP code is not formatted well. What I see:

What it should look like:

MODULE f4_cust_id INPUT.
  TYPES: BEGIN OF TY_INP,
           ID TYPE ZMP_LOGIN-pernr,
         END OF TY_INP.
  DATA: LT_VAL TYPE TABLE OF TY_INP.
  SELECT PERNR FROM ZMP_LOGIN INTO TABLE LT_VAL.
  CALL FUNCTION 'F4IF_INT_TABLE_VALUE_REQUEST'
    EXPORTING
      retfield  = 'CUST_ID'
      dynpprog  = sy-repid
      dynpnr    = sy-dynnr
      VALUE_ORG = 'S'
    TABLES
      value_tab = LT_VAL.
ENDMODULE.

entry.title: Re: Migration/Cut-over handling for open 107/109 Goods-in...
entry.content: Thank you for the feedback. I agree that the cleanest cut-over approach is probably to complete both 107 and 109 in the legacy system before migration, so that valuation and invoice processing are not duplicated in S/4HANA. The remaining issue is the physical availability after go-live. Some containers will still be on the vessel at cut-over, so the stock is financially owned but must not yet be used operationally in the receiving plant. Our idea is to migrate this stock into a dedicated storage location, e.g. “INTR”, which we already plan to use for transit-related processes. The stock should remain relevant for MRP, so that the system recognizes it as available supply and does not create new procurement proposals. However, it should not be available for picking, consumption, production, or sales until the goods physically arrive. As a cut-over activity directly after stock migration, we would create the stock transport order and immediately post the delivery/goods issue, so that the stock is already shown as stock in transit at go-live. When the container physically arrives, we would then post the goods receipt against the stock transport order into the final operational storage location. The purpose is to keep the stock financially migrated and MRP-relevant, but not physically available for operational use until arrival. Has anyone used this kind of approach in an S/4HANA migration cut-over?

entry.title: Lessons Learned from Real SAP S/4HANA Migrations: A Technical Approach to Reducing Risk
entry.content:
Lessons Learned from Real SAP S/4HANA Migrations: A Technical Approach to Reducing Risk

Data migration is one of the most underestimated workstreams in SAP S/4HANA programs, even though it consistently carries some of the highest risk. After leading multiple multi‑region S/4HANA implementations, I’ve seen how quickly data issues can impact testing, cutover stability, and ultimately business continuity. What looks like a “technical activity” on paper often becomes the deciding factor in whether a program lands smoothly or struggles during critical phases.

Key Takeaways

  • Data migration risks are predictable — inconsistent legacy structures, missing attributes, and duplicates appear across nearly every S/4HANA program.
  • Early data profiling is essential — identifying structural gaps during design prevents major SIT/UAT disruptions.
  • Automation reduces manual effort and improves accuracy — record counts, balance checks, and anomaly detection should run every cycle.
  • Multiple mock cycles are critical — two cycles rarely provide enough stability; three or more significantly reduce cutover risk.
  • Centralized monitoring accelerates issue resolution — dashboards and exception logs improve coordination during high‑pressure cutovers.
  • Real project experience matters — successful programs treat data migration as a core transformation stream, not a technical afterthought.

Recurring Technical Challenges I See Across Programs

Across industries and regions, similar technical issues tend to surface:

  • Legacy systems with different data models and inconsistent field usage
  • Mandatory S/4HANA attributes missing in legacy master data
  • Duplicate business partners or materials that break BP conversion logic
  • Undefined reconciliation rules for record counts, balances, and deltas
  • Manual validation steps that don’t scale for high‑volume datasets

These issues aren’t theoretical — they show up in SIT, UAT, and especially during cutover when the pressure is highest.

Why Early Profiling Has Become Non‑Negotiable for Me

One of the biggest shifts in my approach has been pushing data profiling into the design phase. On a recent global rollout, we analyzed KNA1/KNVV data before mapping workshops even started. That early look exposed inconsistent account groups and missing regional assignments across business units. If we had discovered that during mock cycles, the remediation effort would have been far more disruptive.

Early profiling gives the team time to fix structural issues before they become blockers.

Early Data Profiling and Validation Workflow in SAP S/4HANA Migration Programs.

Illustrative example of early profiling, validation, and reconciliation activities performed during enterprise SAP S/4HANA migration cycles.

Anecdote 1: The Material Master Surprise

In another program, we ran an early profiling check on material master data and discovered that nearly 40% of materials across two regions were missing basic units of measure required by S/4HANA. The business assumed these fields were populated because they appeared in legacy reports — but the underlying tables told a different story.
Catching this during design prevented what would have been a major SIT blocker.

Automated Validation Frameworks Make a Measurable Difference

Manual validation will always have a place, but it cannot be the backbone of a large migration. In high‑volume programs, automation becomes essential.

Some of the checks that have consistently helped my teams include:

  • Source vs. target record count validation
  • Financial balance reconciliation (e.g., BSEG vs. ACDOCA)
  • Repeated‑failure detection across cycles
  • Pattern‑based anomaly detection for outliers, invalid dates, or blank clusters

These don’t require a full analytics platform — even lightweight scripts or ETL‑based checks can dramatically reduce manual effort and improve accuracy.

Anecdote 2: The Open Items That Didn’t Add Up

During one S/4HANA finance migration, automated balance checks flagged a recurring mismatch in open items for a specific company code. The business initially believed it was a rounding issue. But because the script highlighted the same discrepancy across three cycles, we dug deeper and found a legacy posting logic that had been quietly creating imbalance for years.
Without automation, this issue would have surfaced only during cutover — and it would have delayed go‑live.

Why I Push for More Migration Cycles Than Most Plans Include

Technically, two cycles look sufficient on a project plan. In reality, they rarely are.

Here’s how cycles typically play out:

  • Cycle 1: You discover the unknown issues
  • Cycle 2: You validate whether the fixes worked
  • Cycle 3+: You stabilize, optimize, and build confidence

The smoothest cutovers I’ve been part of had four or more cycles.

Migration Cycle Stabilization and Reconciliation Trend Example.

Illustrative example showing how repeated migration cycles improve reconciliation accuracy and reduce unresolved issues before cutover.

By the final cycle, the team could predict reconciliation results before the reports even ran — that’s when you know the migration is ready.

Cutover Monitoring: The Technical Backbone of a Calm Go‑Live

During cutover, visibility becomes the most important technical asset. In one multi‑region deployment, we implemented a simple dashboard that consolidated:

  • Failed load records
  • Reconciliation gaps
  • Mapping exceptions
  • Outstanding validation errors

It wasn’t a complex tool — but it transformed how quickly teams could respond. Instead of searching for information, they focused on resolving issues.

Sample Cutover Monitoring and Exception Tracking Dashboard.

Illustrative example of centralized monitoring used to track reconciliation gaps, failed loads, and validation exceptions during cutover activities.

That single change reduced cutover stress significantly.

Closing Thoughts

From a technical practitioner’s perspective, data migration is where the transformation meets real‑world complexity. Tools matter, but they don’t replace disciplined processes. What consistently reduces risk in S/4HANA programs is:

  • Early data profiling
  • Automated validation
  • Iterative migration cycles
  • Centralized monitoring and exception handling

These practices have made a measurable difference in every program I’ve led, and they continue to be the foundation of stable, predictable cutovers.

entry.title: Re: GL Role Restriction based on Company Code.
entry.content: Hi @ksiddiqui, as mentioned before, the screenshot I've previously sent was just for illustration. The configuration for hierarchies depends on your business rules, each company has its own, that’s why some have to use the KBA instructions to set restrictions properly. Please follow the resolution of this KBA(https://me.sap.com/notes/0003595981) --> Setting "Value Help" to "Restricted" [just like you did before with the other ones]. -> On the "General" tab set the company code restriction [as you've already performed on the write and read] -> Search for Company Code Hierarchies restriction [use the search tool below "Assigned Restriction Type"] -> To Maintain Restriction on the options "Company Code Hierarchy" and "Company Code Hierarchy Node" edit and select the company codes to restrict [click on the pencil on the right of each option.] [As on the right top corner you only have the options "Unrestricted" and "NOT Maintained" you must do it manually] -> Save.

entry.title: Re: I want to know how to resolve real-time revenue errors.
entry.content:
Hello @db11 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your process, the root cause is most likely change you made to the account assignment during supplier invoice approval. The supplier invoice item is originally linked to the customer project but the approver changes the invoice detail and assign a fixed asset. This creates an account assignment situation that is not valid for real-time revenue recognition.

To put it in simple terms:

  • Event-based revenue recognition works based on posting to the relevant project account assignment object.
  • Postings to an account assignment object are considered by event-based revenue recognition.
  • A fixed asset acquisition is an Asset Accounting process, not a normal project cost posting for revenue recognition.
  • If the supplier invoice line is changed to an asset account assignment, real-time revenue recognition cannot handle it as a normal customer project cost event.
  • Reprocessing may allow the period-end process to continue but it does not remove the root cause. Note that Manage Real-Time Revenue Recognition Issues is used to view errors from automatic revenue recognition, for example when master data or process data causes an issue.

The clean way to handle it is to prevent supplier invoice items that are relevant for customer project revenue recognition from being changed to a fixed asset account assignment during approval. You should decide the business intent of the supplier invoice item if they are any of these:

  1. It is a project cost: If the supplier invoice is part of the customer project cost, keep the account assignment on the customer project/work package/WBS-related project object, depending on the app and process you use. Do not change the same invoice item to a fixed asset during approval.
  2. It is an asset acquisition: Do not treat it as a cost posting for customer project revenue recognition.
  3. It must be both project-related and capitalised: Then this should be designed as a controlled project capitalisation process, not by manually changing the supplier invoice approval line to a fixed asset account assignment. For this case, check whether your activate scope supports the required investment or asset capitalisation flow. If not, raise an SAP incident with the exact error details.

The monthly error will continue as long as the system still finds postings with an unsupported account assignment context for real-time revenue recognition. You can stop this by:

  • Identifying the supplier invoice items causing the error in Manage Real-Time Revenue Recognition issues.       
  • Checking the accounting document in Manage Journal Entries.                                                   
  • Confirm whether the line has fixed asset assignment instead of the intended project assignment.
  • Reverse and repost the supplier invoice correctly if the posting is wrong.
  • Adjust the supplier invoice approval process so approvers do not change project-relevant invoice lines to fixed asset assignment.
  • If the asset assignment is required by business design, exclude that posting from the customer project revenue recognition flow and process it through Asset Accounting instead.

Here are references for more guiding details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/afee0280b4c44b44b118634a87f9d3a7.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/6d56b5a5cf5d481d88097dc9e34f0a2d.html?locale=en-US

3041610 - Explanation for buttons in Revenue Recognition Fiori Apps - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Issues in Perform Maintenance Jobs
entry.content:
Hi Experts!

I have a few questions about this application. Questions 1 and 2 are particularly critical for me as they are affecting the process.  Thank you very much in advance.

1. When I try to record time confirmation using "SA" (Saat, which means Hour), the system gives the following error:

"Unit of measure H has not been created in language TR."

However, we do not use "H". We use the Turkish unit "SA" for hours. 

What is interesting is that the system allows me to save confirmations in Turkish when using dakika(DAK- which means minute). 

Also, if I  change the unit to another unit of measure and then switch back to "SA" (hours), the system allows the save successfully.

This behavior makes me think the issue may not actually be related to the missing of unit "H" in Turkish, because i can actually save with sa. Additionally, I do not receive this error when I select English as the system language or using IW41

2. When I try to enter a measurement and click save, the system does not save it, and I also cannot see the measurement entry in IK18.  When I save  measurement entry in another project, it is saved successfully. In this system, I was not able to activate the WDA measurement applications. Could this be related?

3. When I try to return part of a planned material, the system adds a new line below, and this line is also added to the order.

When I enter the quantity to be returned, the system does not immediately process or update it, and it still appears as 0. 

However, after entering the return quantity in the order, the “Post” button for the return line becomes active in the application, allowing me to complete the return.

Is this the expected process flow, or is there an issue here?

4.  I also receive this warning when using unplanned materials;

Is there really no way to return the material in this scenario? There should still be some way to process a return, shouldn’t there?

entry.title: Variable Pay (overall rating from indv. rating, company and department)
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

we are currently facing the following challenge in Variable Pay:

Our setup consists of Company Goals, Department/divison Goals, and an Individual Rating based on objectives + performance rating (pm form)

From these values, we calculate an overall achievement score, which is then used to derive payout guidelines.

Option 1:
We integrate both Company Goals and Department Goals in PMGM form and derive the overall achievement score there. This would allow us to easily connect the result to guidelines.

The challenge with this approach is handling mid-year organizational changes, where Department Goals cannot always be assigned correctly based on employee criteria. 

Option 2:
We map Company Goals and Department Goals directly in Variable Pay and calculate the overall achievement score using formulas. Based on this result, we would recreate the guideline logic with additional formulas and validation.

Has anyone implemented a similar setup or are there alternative approaches we should consider @xavierlegarrec ?

entry.title: From XSUAA to AMS: Architecting S4HANA CAP Extensions with Policy-Based Authorization
entry.content:
Introduction

In modern SAP landscapes, extending S/4HANA is no longer just about building services or exposing APIs. The real complexity lies in how to secure enterprise extensions in a scalable and maintainable way.

Traditionally, SAP applications on SAP BTP have relied on XSUAA (XSUAA OAuth service) for authentication and authorization. While this model works well for scope-based access control, enterprise requirements often go beyond simple roles. They often involve business-context-based restrictions such as country, company code, or organizational unit.

As many of us, I have extensively used XSUAA to secure enterprise applications in SAP landscapes. In one of my recent projects, I had the opportunity to work with the IAS + AMS-based authentication and authorization model. That is when the WHY behind this architecture shift became clear.

As always, I wanted to share my learnings through a practical example - starting with the core concepts, followed by architectural differences and then a hands-on implementation.

The GOAL was not just to build another CAP application, but to understand a key architectural shift.

Let's get started by understanding the core design differences between the two authorization strategies

Core Architecture Differences

In SAP BTP - 
  • XSUAA has traditionally been the standard service for securing applications using scopes, role templates and role collections.
  • With AMS, the security model shifts toward a clearer separation between identity and authorization.

Let's start by understanding each model and key ideas behind it.

XSUAA Mental Model

Following diagram shows how the XSUAA model works in a simple way 

Figure-1

Key Ideas

  • IAS is the Identity Provider (IdP) responsible for authenticating users and establishing their identity.
  • XSUAA is the OAuth Authorization Server for the application, which issues JWT tokens containing scopes.
  • Authorization is scope-driven, where access control is enforced using scopes, role templates and role collections embedded inside the JWT token consumed by the application.

AMS Mental Model

Following diagram shows how the AMS model works in a simple way

Figure-2

 

Key Ideas

  • IAS is the Identity Provider (IdP) responsible for authenticating users and establishing identity.
  • AMS is the policy-based Authorization Engine that evaluates access rules based on roles, attributes and business conditions.
  • Authorization is policy-driven, where access is determined using configurable policies (e.g., country, company code) instead of static scopes.

Responsibility Shift

This comparison highlights a fundamental shift in how authorization is modeled: From static scopes defined at design time to dynamic policies evaluated at runtime.

AspectXSUAAAMS
Configuration filexs-security.json*.dcl
Authorization building blocksRoles + ScopesRoles + Policies + Attributes
Instance based data restrictionsLeads to role explosionStable roles + Dynamic policies
Change impactRedeploy neededPolicy update 
Runtime evaluationJWT scopesPolicy-driven (via ams plugin)

To make this architecture concrete, let’s walk through a simple vendor onboarding application that demonstrates how IAS, AMS and CAP work together in practice.

End-To-End Implementation: Hands-On

I have built a side-by-side extension - Vendor Onboarding application where:

  • Requesters can create and view vendor onboarding requests
  • Reviewers can approve or reject requests with comments
  • Role-based visibility is enforced using AMS policies to restrict data access

The following solution diagram illustrates the main components of the application

Figure-3

The above solution diagram illustrates the end-to-end architecture of the Vendor Onboarding application, including the CAP service, App Router, IAS authentication, AMS-based authorization and the SAP HANA persistence layer. It provides a high-level view of how the components interact in the overall system.

The complete GitHub repository for this implementation is publicly available and contains detailed documentation covering all aspects of the solution. In following sections, we will walk through some of the key AMS-related concepts and configurations used in this implementation.

AMS Authorization Policies

  • The cds add ams command generates an ams/dcl/ folder at the project root:
ams/dcl/
  schema.dcl                - AMS attribute schema (auto-generated; user-editable)
  cap/
    basePolicies.dcl        - auto-generated by cds build — DO NOT edit
  vendor/onboarding/
    reviewerPolicies.dcl    - hand-written app-based policies
  • Adding @restrict at the entity level is what triggers CAP's AMS plugin to generate basePolicies.dcl
@restrict: [
  { grant: ['READ', 'CREATE'], to: 'VendorRequester', where: 'requestedBy = $user' },
  { grant: ['READ', 'UPDATE', 'DELETE'], to: 'VendorReviewer' }
]
  • cds build translates this into
POLICY "VendorRequester" {
  ASSIGN ROLE "VendorRequester";
}

POLICY "VendorReviewer" {
  ASSIGN ROLE "VendorReviewer";
}

Instance Based Policies

To restrict VendorReviewers to specific countries, instance-based authorization is achieved by combining AMS schema attributes with policy definitions.
  • Defining AMS schema attributes: The first step is to expose relevant business fields as AMS attributes using CDS annotations. In this case, the country field is marked as an authorization attribute as shown below
@AMS.attributes: { country: (country) }
entity VendorRequests as projection on db.VendorRequests { ... }​
  • cds build then generates schema.dcl with: This registers country as a condition attribute usable in AMS policies.
SCHEMA {
  country: String
}
  • Write instance policies in ams/dcl/vendor/onboarding/reviewerPolicies.dcl:
POLICY "VendorReviewer_US" {
  ASSIGN ROLE "VendorReviewer" WHERE country = 'US';
}

POLICY "VendorReviewer_CA" {
  ASSIGN ROLE "VendorReviewer" WHERE country = 'CA';
}
 
This model enables fine-grained, instance-based authorization where the same CAP role (VendorReviewer) behaves differently depending on the policy context (e.g., country).

Instead of creating multiple roles per country, AMS allows authorization rules to be externalized into policies, improving scalability and maintainability.

The repository README contains the complete implementation details and configuration steps for this setup.

Wrap-Up

In this blog, we explored a practical implementation of a SAP CAP-based Vendor Onboarding extension secured using IAS and AMS and compared it with the traditional XSUAA-based security model.

The key objective was not just to build an application, but to understand the architectural shift in how enterprise authorization is modeled on SAP BTP.

With XSUAA, authorization is primarily scope-driven, where roles, role templates and role collections define access boundaries. While this model works well for standard role-based access control, it becomes rigid when enterprise requirements demand contextual restrictions such as country, organization, or cost center.

Disclaimer: This is not an official reference application or documentation. The thoughts outlined in this blog are based on my real world experience and learnings.

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/java/ams
  2. https://learning.sap.com/courses/exploring-the-fundamentals-of-sap-system-security/introducing-sap-cloud-identity-services 
  3. https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/data-control-language-dcl

entry.title: Re: Reusable validation framework approach in UI5
entry.content:
Great write-up, Pooja!

This is a clean and practical approach to solving one of the most common UI5 pain points—duplicate validation logic scattered across multiple controllers. Moving the validation rules into a dedicated ValidationHelper.js not only improves reusability but also enforces better separation of concerns, which is something many UI5 projects struggle with.

A few things I especially liked:

  • Use of findAggregatedObjects to dynamically detect required fields. This keeps the solution generic and avoids hard‑coding control IDs.

  • Simple, readable API — validateRequiredFields(view) is intuitive and easy for any controller to adopt.

  • ValueState handling is consistent and user-friendly, which is key for form-heavy applications.

If you plan to extend this further, you might consider adding:

  • Pattern-based validations (email, phone formats, numeric ranges)

  • A central configuration file for validation rules

  • Support for custom messages per field

  • Integration with message popovers for multi-field error summaries

Overall, this is a solid, reusable pattern that many UI5 developers—especially those working on large enterprise apps—will find valuable. Thanks for sharing this!

entry.title: Re: What is the Use of Private Environment in SAP Build P...
entry.content:
The private environment is basically your own isolated workspace for SAP Build Process Automation. It lets you deploy and run projects with tighter security, controlled access, and separate resources instead of sharing everything in the public/default environment.

Why it matters

A private or shared environment is useful when different teams, business areas, or projects should not see or use the same artifacts, triggers, agents, or API keys. SAP’s documentation says environments are the functional area where you deploy and run projects, and shared environments increase security by limiting access and managing project members.

In practical terms

  • You can keep automation projects separated by business domain, such as finance, procurement, or HR.

  • You can assign only the right users, agents, and artifacts to that environment.

  • You can deploy different project versions in different environments without mixing them up.

When to use it

Use a private/shared environment when you want better governance, safer testing, or clean separation between projects and teams. It is especially helpful when you do not want every user in the tenant to access the same processes or automations.

entry.title: Re: Convergent Invoicing SSCUIs not visible in Configurat...
entry.content:
Hi byd,
thank you for your detailed questions and for describing your scenario clearly. Let me address your points below:

a) SSCUIs availability in CBC
The SSCUIs you listed are part of the Convergent Invoicing (CI) configuration scope.

These SSCUIs generally become available once the relevant Convergent Invoicing scope is activated. In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, this is typically linked to scope item 2BG. After activation of 2BG, the majority of the SSCUIs you listed should become visible in CBC.
An exception to this is 103286 – Define Residence Time for Billable Items. This is not exposed in CBC in the same way as the others and therefore will remain unavailable.


b) Role of Convergent Invoicing & your manufacturing use case
Your understanding of Convergent Invoicing is technically correct in principle: It consolidates multiple billing sources into a single invoice.

However, in the context of S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, there is an important product direction to be aware of:
Currently, there are no plans to support integration from Sales Billing to Convergent Invoicing in S/4HANA Public Cloud. The business need to combine physical goods and subscription or service charges is already covered by standard Sales Billing capabilities.

Relevant scope items for this scenario are Omnichannel Convergent Billing (1MC) & Subscription Management with Sales Billing (57Z). These scope items allow:
- Integration of subscription and service billing directly into SD billing
- Creation of combined invoices within SD

For more information please review influence request 294573 - SD to SAP CI integration to create convergent invoice in S/4HANA Public Cloud.


Best regards,
Daniel

entry.title: VIES Validation with Multiple VAT IDs
entry.content:
SAP Support Request: VIES Validation with Multiple VAT IDs

Affected Component Component: FI-LOC-OVF (Online Validation Framework) Subcomponent: VIES Validation

Problem Description Brief Summary: SSCUI 105630 allows only one VAT ID for VIES validation with multiple subsidiaries

Detailed Description: Our company has multiple subsidiaries in various EU countries, which are represented by separate company codes in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud. Each subsidiary has its own VAT identification number. However, the VIES validation system in SSCUI 105630 "Maintain VIES specific parameters" only allows the storage of a single "Requestor's VAT Number". This leads to limitations in proper VAT validation of EU business partners, as not all subsidiaries can be correctly represented as requestors. A valid requestor VAT ID is required for VIES requests to obtain consultation numbers.   

How could we deal with this? Are there already solutions for it?

  • Note: There does not appear to be an associated Customizing option for this functionality (AFAIK)

entry.title: Re: LMS notifications
entry.content: Just adding on top of what Duda has suggested, in some cases we have also observe the behaviour of footer image needing to be downloaded or permitted due to the "From" email notification server has not been whitelisted by the organisation's IT/Security team. Some information can be found by here (https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-learning/managing-sap-successfactors-learning-for-administrators/email-domain-validation-using-domain-keys-identified-mail-dkim?locale=en-US&version=LATEST), or search SuccessFactors Learning DKIM, which should help on the set up. Thanks, Sunny

entry.title: Contextualize and reason Post-SAP Sapphire SAP Business Data Cloud briefing
entry.content:
AI pilots are easy.  Scaling trusted AI across the enterprise is where the real work starts.

Join our post-SAP Sapphire briefing for SAP Partners and learn how SAP Business Data Cloud powers the Autonomous Enterprise.

We’re covering:
• SAP BDC and Knowledge Graph
• Joule and Joule Studio
• AI Agent Hub and partner motions
• Faster migrations and governed AI
• New SELL, SERVICE, and BUILD opportunities

Plus, we’ll connect the technology to customer outcomes that matter: lower complexity, smarter automation, and faster decision-making across SAP and non-SAP systems.

One detail that stands out?  Customers are asking for AI they can trust, not disconnected copilots that create more noise.

This session helps you position the bigger story and turn it into action with your customers.

Register now and bring the Autonomous Enterprise conversation into every account.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/may/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014197.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPTechEd #AIAgents #SAPData #FutureOfEnterprise
